content.p: Use the Limit Comparison Test to determine if \renewcommand{\log}{\ln} \displaystyle \sum_{ n= {{k0}} }^\infty \frac{ {{top}} }{ {{bottom}} } converges.

outtro.p:
Let \renewcommand{\log}{\ln} \displaystyle a_n = \frac{ {{top}} }{ {{bottom}} }, b_n = \frac{1}{n^{ {{P}} } }. Note that a_n is a positive, eventually decreasing sequence. We have that:
\renewcommand{\log}{\ln} \displaystyle \lim_{n\to\infty} \frac{ {{top}} }{ {{bottom}} } \cdot \frac{ n^{ {{P}} } }{1} = \lim_{n\to\infty} \sqrt{\frac{ {{topsquare}} }{ {{bottomsquare}} } \cdot \frac{n^{ {{Psquare}} }}{1} } = \sqrt{\lim_{n\to\infty}\frac{ {{final}} }{ {{bottomsquare}} } } = {{simpcoeff}}. We can see that 0 < {{simpcoeff}} < \infty so by the Limit Comparison Test, \renewcommand{\log}{\ln} \displaystyle \sum_{ n= {{k0}} }^\infty \frac{ {{top}} }{ {{bottom}} } converges if and only if \renewcommand{\log}{\ln} \displaystyle \sum_{ n= {{k0}} }^\infty \frac{ 1 }{ n^{ {{P}} } } converges, which it does by the p-test since {{P}} > 1. So \renewcommand{\log}{\ln} \displaystyle \sum_{ n= {{k0}} }^\infty \frac{ {{top}} }{ {{bottom}} } converges.

content.p: Use the Limit Comparison Test to determine if \renewcommand{\log}{\ln} \displaystyle \sum_{ n= {{k0}} }^\infty \frac{ {{top}} }{ {{bottom}} } converges.

outtro.p:
Let \renewcommand{\log}{\ln} \displaystyle a_n = \frac{ {{top}} }{ {{bottom}} }, b_n = \frac{1}{n^{ {{P}} } }. Note that a_n is a positive, eventually decreasing sequence. We have that:
\renewcommand{\log}{\ln} \displaystyle \lim_{n\to\infty} \frac{ {{top}} }{ {{bottom}} } \cdot \frac{ n^{ {{P}} } }{1} = \lim_{n\to\infty} \sqrt{\frac{ {{topsquare}} }{ {{bottomsquare}} } \cdot \frac{n^{ {{Psquare}} }}{1} } = \sqrt{\lim_{n\to\infty}\frac{ {{final}} }{ {{bottomsquare}} } } = {{simpcoeff}}. We can see that 0 < {{simpcoeff}} < \infty so by the Limit Comparison Test, \renewcommand{\log}{\ln} \displaystyle \sum_{ n= {{k0}} }^\infty \frac{ {{top}} }{ {{bottom}} } converges if and only if \renewcommand{\log}{\ln} \displaystyle \sum_{ n= {{k0}} }^\infty \frac{ 1 }{ n^{ {{P}} } } converges, which it does not by the p-test since {{P}} \leq 1. So \renewcommand{\log}{\ln} \displaystyle \sum_{ n= {{k0}} }^\infty \frac{ {{top}} }{ {{bottom}} } diverges.

content.p: Use the Limit Comparison Test to determine if \renewcommand{\log}{\ln} \displaystyle \sum_{ n= {{k0}} }^\infty \left( {{Fn}} \right) converges.

outtro.p:
Let \renewcommand{\log}{\ln} \displaystyle a_n = {{Fn}} , b_n = \frac{1}{n^{ {{P}} } }. Note that a_n is a positive, eventually decreasing sequence. We have that:
\renewcommand{\log}{\ln} \displaystyle \lim_{n\to\infty} \frac{ {{Fn}} }{ \frac{1}{ n^{ {{P}} } } } = \lim_{n\to\infty} \frac{ {{Fd}} }{ {{Pd}} } = {{limit}}. (Not all work shown.) We can see that \renewcommand{\log}{\ln} 0 < {{limit}} < \infty so by the Limit Comparison Test, \renewcommand{\log}{\ln} \displaystyle \sum_{ n= {{k0}} }^\infty \left( {{Fn}} \right) converges if and only if \renewcommand{\log}{\ln} \displaystyle \sum_{ n= {{k0}} }^\infty \frac{ 1 }{ n^{ {{P}} } } converges, which it does by the p-test since {{P}} > 1. So \renewcommand{\log}{\ln} \displaystyle \sum_{ n= {{k0}} }^\infty\left( {{Fn}} \right) converges.

content.p: Use the Limit Comparison Test to determine if \renewcommand{\log}{\ln} \displaystyle \sum_{ n= {{k0}} }^\infty \left( {{Fn}} \right) converges.

outtro.p:
Let \renewcommand{\log}{\ln} \displaystyle a_n = {{Fn}} , b_n = \frac{1}{n^{ {{P}} } }. Note that a_n is a positive, eventually decreasing sequence. We have that:
\renewcommand{\log}{\ln} \displaystyle \lim_{n\to\infty} \frac{ {{Fn}} }{ \frac{1}{ n^{ {{P}} } } } = \lim_{n\to\infty} \frac{ {{Fd}} }{ {{Pd}} } = {{limit}}. (Not all work shown.) We can see that \renewcommand{\log}{\ln} 0 < {{limit}} < \infty so by the Limit Comparison Test, \renewcommand{\log}{\ln} \displaystyle \sum_{ n= {{k0}} }^\infty \left( {{Fn}} \right) converges if and only if \renewcommand{\log}{\ln} \displaystyle \sum_{ n= {{k0}} }^\infty \frac{ 1 }{ n^{ {{P}} } } converges, which it does not by the p-test since {{P}} \leq 1. So \renewcommand{\log}{\ln} \displaystyle \sum_{ n= {{k0}} }^\infty \left( {{Fn}} \right) diverges.
